metadata.title: Build an Avoided Crossing Plot with Claude Code
metadata.color_semantics: TEAL = upper energy branch E+. CRIMSON = lower energy branch E-. INK for all Text.

beats.narration_text: This is Liam, in for Bear. Medhavy A-I. From Sanskrit — meaning intelligent, or intellectually brilliant. The perfect name for our A-I-powered intelligent learning systems.

lines: — the perfect name for our AI-powered

beats.narration_text: The prompt. Claude Code terminal: claude — plot the energy levels of a two-state quantum system as a function of detuning lambda. E-one equals zero, E-two equals lambda. Coupling V equals zero-point-one electron-volts. Show both unperturbed dashed lines crossing freely, and the perturbed eigenvalues that avoid the crossing. Mark the minimum gap of two times V equals zero-point-two electron-volts at the degeneracy point. Lambda from zero to two electron-volts.

props.command:
claude "plot avoided crossing for two-level system:
  E₁=0, E₂=λ, coupling V=0.1 eV
  λ ∈ [0, 2] eV
  show: dashed V=0 crossing lines
  show: perturbed E± bending apart
  mark: min gap = 2|V| = 0.2 eV"

beats.narration_text: The CLI writes it. Read before running. Two checks. First: the eigenvalue formula. The perturbed energies are E-plus-minus equals the mean of E-one and E-two, plus or minus the square root of the half-gap squared plus V-squared. At the degeneracy point where lambda equals one, the square root collapses to just V. The gap is exactly two times V. Second: the code should compute this analytically, not numerically — if the code just plots two straight lines, it found the unperturbed levels, not the dressed states. Thirty seconds.

props.code:
# avoided_crossing.py  —  Claude Code output
import numpy as np

# Two-level system: E1=0, E2=lam, coupling V
V = 0.1  # eV
lam = np.linspace(0, 2, 300)

E1_0, E2_0 = 0 * lam, lam          # unperturbed (cross freely)
mean  = (E1_0 + E2_0) / 2
half  = (E2_0 - E1_0) / 2

# Perturbed eigenvalues — exact 2×2 diagonalization
E_plus  = mean + np.sqrt(half**2 + V**2)   # upper branch
E_minus = mean - np.sqrt(half**2 + V**2)   # lower branch
# At lam=1: gap = E_plus - E_minus = 2V = 0.2 eV

beats.narration_text: Run it. Two checks. First: the teal upper branch and the crimson lower branch. At lambda far from one, they run nearly flat and nearly linear — the system is close to one of its unperturbed states. Near lambda equals one, they bend away from each other. That bend is repulsion. Second: the minimum gap. The double-headed arrow at lambda equals one shows zero-point-two electron-volts — exactly two times V. No matter how slowly you tune through the crossing, the system stays on one branch. The levels never touch. The von Neumann–Wigner theorem. Every coupled two-level system in physics — a qubit, a chemical bond, a laser-dressed atom — lives on one of these two curves.

Iterate: claude — update avoided-crossing dot py — set V to zero-point-three, replot, confirm the gap doubles to zero-point-six. One number, one diff, one re-run. The gap scales linearly with coupling strength. That is the testable prediction. Prompt, read, run, check, change. The avoided crossing was the vehicle. The workflow is the takeaway.

props.command:
claude "update avoided_crossing.py:
  → set V=0.3 eV
  → confirm gap = 2V = 0.6 eV at λ=1"
